title: From Independent to Correlated Diffusion: Generalized Generative Modeling with Probabilistic Computers
abstract: Diffusion models have emerged as a powerful framework for generative tasks in deep learning. They decompose generative modeling into two computational primitives: deterministic neural-network evaluation and stochastic sampling. Current implementations usually place most computation in the neural network, but diffusion as a framework allows a broader range of choices for the stochastic transition kernel. Here, we generalize the stochastic sampling component by replacing independent noise injection with Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) dynamics that incorporate known interaction structure. Standard independent diffusion is recovered as a special case when couplings are set to zero. By explicitly incorporating Ising couplings into the diffusion dynamics, the noising and denoising processes exploit spatial correlations representative of the target system. The resulting framework maps naturally onto probabilistic computers (p-computers) built from probabilistic bits (p-bits), which provide orders-of-magnitude advantages in sampling throughput and energy efficiency over GPUs. We demonstrate the approach on equilibrium states of the 2D ferromagnetic Ising model and the 3D Edwards-Anderson spin glass, showing that correlated diffusion produces samples in closer agreement with MCMC reference distributions than independent diffusion. More broadly, the framework shows that p-computers can enable new classes of diffusion algorithms that exploit structured probabilistic sampling for generative modeling.

sections.heading: I.  Introduction
sections.text:
Diffusion models are a class of generative models inspired by non-equilibrium thermodynamics [ 1 ] .
They operate through a forward stochastic noising process that progressively corrupts data, a training phase in which a neural network learns to reverse this corruption, and inference , in which new samples are generated by iteratively applying learned, stochastic denoising transitions [ 2 , 3 ] .
The probabilistic nature of diffusion models means they are inherently composed of two computational components: deterministic neural-network evaluation (a conditional estimator) and stochastic sampling (a transition kernel that realizes the next state).

A central but often overlooked aspect of diffusion models is that their algorithmic structure imposes no fundamental constraint on how computational effort is divided between these two components.
In current practice, the widespread availability of GPUs has pushed most of the cost into the neural-network evaluation, which is well matched to dense linear algebra, while the stochastic sampling is typically kept lightweight to fit what this hardware makes easiest to execute [ 4 ] .
This is a hardware-contingent design choice rather than a property of the diffusion framework, and it leaves underexplored a complementary regime in which the sampling step is fast enough to justify richer, more informative transition kernels.

Probabilistic computers (p-computers) built from probabilistic bits (p-bits) [ 5 , 6 ] and their Gaussian counterparts (g-bits) provide exactly this capability.
These p-bits are binary stochastic units that naturally sample from Ising-type energy landscapes, and networks of p-bits implement Gibbs sampling of distributions defined by programmable couplings and biases.
They thus constitute a form of Ising machine [ 7 , 8 ] : hardware designed to minimize Ising Hamiltonians, but extended here to support controllable stochastic sampling rather than energy minimization alone.
Probabilistic computers built from p-bits have demonstrated strong performance in combinatorial optimization and machine learning [ 9 , 10 , 11 ] , with native hardware implementations on FPGAs and projected stochastic magnetic tunnel junction (sMTJ) devices offering orders-of-magnitude advantages in sampling throughput and energy efficiency over conventional GPU-based implementations [ 10 ] (quantified in Section II , Table 1 ).

The mapping of diffusion onto p-computers is particularly relevant because recent proposals for energy-based candidate selection [ 12 ] and Monte Carlo tree search [ 13 , 14 ] during inference further increase the demand for fast, efficient probabilistic sampling.

For physical systems governed by known interactions, such as Ising models with coupling coefficients J i ​ j J_{ij} , there is a further opportunity: the sampling distributions within diffusion can be constructed directly from the correlations of the underlying system.
Rather than sampling from independent noise at each diffusion step, the stochastic component can perform structure-aware Gibbs dynamics that respect the physical couplings.
We show that allowing diffusion to sample from these physically motivated distributions improves the accuracy of generated samples, and that independent diffusion is recovered as a special case when the couplings are set to zero ( J i ​ j = 0 J_{ij}=0 ).

For systems with known couplings, direct MCMC sampling is available but can require prohibitively long chains to decorrelate, particularly near phase transitions or in frustrated systems. Correlated diffusion offers an alternative route: once trained, the hybrid neural-network/Gibbs reverse process can generate decorrelated approximate samples intended to match the target distribution without requiring long equilibrium chains at inference time. In this sense, the framework is analogous to a Boltzmann generator [ 15 ] for discrete systems, where the neural network proposes global denoising moves and the p-bit sampler enforces local consistency. Recent work on augmenting MCMC with learned proposals [ 16 ] demonstrates the value of this approach; correlated diffusion extends it by embedding the physical structure directly into the generative dynamics.

In this work, we formulate a generalized diffusion framework that incorporates Ising-structured Gibbs dynamics into both the forward noising and reverse inference processes.
The framework naturally maps onto a hybrid architecture (Fig. 1 ) in which p-computers handle the stochastic sampling and GPUs handle the neural-network-based conditional estimation.
More broadly, any Ising machine capable of controllable stochastic sampling (e.g., Ref. [ 7 , 17 , 18 , 19 , 20 ] ) can serve as the sampling backend in this framework.
We introduce the probabilistic computing primitives, p-bits and g-bits (Fig. 2 ), that implement these dynamics, and quantify their sampling efficiency relative to GPUs (Table 1 ).
We demonstrate the approach on two benchmark systems: the 2D ferromagnetic Ising model and the 3D Edwards-Anderson spin glass, showing that correlated diffusion produces samples that align more closely with MCMC reference distributions of equilibrium observables.

sections.heading: II.  Probabilistic computing primitives: p-bits and g-bits
sections.text:
Before developing the diffusion framework, we introduce the probabilistic computing primitives on which it is built.

The p-bit [ 5 , 6 ] is a binary stochastic unit whose output s i ∈ { − 1 , + 1 } s_{i}\in\{-1,+1\} is sampled according to s i = sgn ⁡ [ tanh ⁡ ( β ​ I i ) + rand ⁡ ( − 1 , 1 ) ] s_{i}=\operatorname{sgn}\!\big[\tanh(\beta\,I_{i})+\operatorname{rand}(-1,1)\big] (1) where rand ⁡ ( − 1 , 1 ) \operatorname{rand}(-1,1) denotes a uniform random number on ( − 1 , 1 ) (-1,1) and β \beta is the inverse temperature controlling the degree of stochasticity. The local field I i I_{i} seen by each p-bit is determined by its neighbors through I i = ∑ j J i ​ j ​ s j + h i I_{i}=\sum_{j}J_{ij}\,s_{j}+h_{i} (2) where J i ​ j J_{ij} are pairwise couplings and h i h_{i} is an external bias. A network of p-bits connected by couplings J i ​ j J_{ij} samples from the Ising energy E ​ ( 𝐬 ) = − ∑ i < j J i ​ j ​ s i ​ s j − ∑ i h i ​ s i E(\mathbf{s})=-\sum_{i<j}J_{ij}\,s_{i}\,s_{j}-\sum_{i}h_{i}\,s_{i} (3) At high inverse temperature ( β → ∞ \beta\to\infty ), each p-bit becomes nearly deterministic, settling into the state favored by its local field; at low inverse temperature ( β → 0 \beta\to 0 ), the tanh \tanh term vanishes and the p-bit outputs become completely random. A decreasing β \beta schedule therefore provides a natural mechanism for progressively degrading a structured configuration into uncorrelated noise.

p-bits can be physically realized using a variety of stochastic nanodevices, including stochastic magnetic tunnel junctions (sMTJs) [ 9 , 21 , 22 , 23 ] , Zener diodes [ 24 ] , and single-photon avalanche diodes (SPADs) [ 25 ] (Fig. 2 c).

While p-bits are binary, many generative models, including Gaussian diffusion [ 1 ] and Gaussian-Bernoulli Restricted Boltzmann Machines [ 26 , 27 ] , require multi-state stochastic units. g-bits [ 28 ] fill this role: they are tunable Gaussian stochastic units that can be implemented either natively, using internal device physics to produce multi-state outputs directly, or as networks of interconnected p-bits that collectively represent 2 N 2^{N} states (Fig. 2 ). Networks of coupled g-bits sample from a quadratic energy landscape with programmable means, variances, and pairwise couplings, analogous to the Ising energy for p-bits (see Supplementary Section VII.1 for the g-bit energy and Gibbs sampling equations, and Supplementary Section VII.2 for the internal p-bit representation). Since diffusion models have two primary variants, Bernoulli and Gaussian [ 1 ] , they map directly onto p-bits and g-bits, respectively, making p-computers broadly applicable to generative AI workloads.

To quantify the hardware advantage, we compare the efficiency of 32-bit random sample generation across GPU, FPGA, and projected stochastic magnetic tunnel junction (sMTJ) platforms. We emphasize that these efficiency figures refer to raw stochastic sample generation, not full end-to-end diffusion inference, however, they are directly relevant for correlated diffusion because its reverse process shifts computational cost toward repeated Gibbs sampling. Throughout this section, a “sample” refers to one 32-bit random number produced by the underlying stochastic hardware primitive, not a full Ising configuration or a complete Gibbs sweep. The FPGA implementation uses 32-bit linear feedback shift registers (LFSRs) to generate uniformly distributed random numbers, an approach validated for MCMC sampling and optimization [ 10 , 29 ] .
GPU numbers are from our A100 experiments, informed by published benchmarks [ 30 , 31 , 32 , 33 ] . FPGA numbers are from an AMD Alveo board, and sMTJ projections draw upon experimental results and scaling from Refs. [ 21 , 22 , 23 , 9 ] . All numbers are order-of-magnitude consistent across sources (see Supplementary Section X for details). In proposed projections, sMTJs can be fabricated directly on top of CMOS circuitry with minimal wiring delay, offering substantial area and energy advantages [ 29 ] .
As shown in Table 1 , FPGA-based p-computers already achieve ∼ 10 2 × \sim\!10^{2}\times the sampling efficiency of GPUs, and projected sMTJ-based implementations extend this advantage to ∼ 10 4 × \sim\!10^{4}\times over GPUs. Detailed hardware specifications are provided in Supplementary Section IX (Table S1 ), the FPGA p-bit architecture is described in Supplementary Section VIII , and the GPU benchmarking methodology is detailed in Supplementary Section X . These efficiency gains become particularly significant for physics-based applications that require MCMC sampling. In the present implementation, one generated sample requires approximately N chains ​ ∑ t = 1 T ( t − 1 ) N_{\mathrm{chains}}\sum_{t=1}^{T}(t-1) forward Gibbs sweeps. For T = 100 T=100 and N chains = 10 N_{\mathrm{chains}}=10 , this corresponds to 49 , 500 49{,}500 Gibbs sweeps per generated sample, making sampling with dedicated probabilistic computers directly relevant for reducing end-to-end inference costs.

With these primitives in hand, diffusion steps can be interpreted as scheduled stochastic dynamics. A single p-bit with a time-dependent local bias h i ( t ) h_{i}^{(t)} and J i ​ j = 0 J_{ij}=0 implements a 2 × 2 2\times 2 Markov transition corresponding to independent noise injection at each site. A network of N N interacting p-bits, by contrast, realizes a 2 N × 2 N 2^{N}\times 2^{N} transition matrix that captures the full correlation structure of the Ising Hamiltonian.

In Section III , we show how the independent diffusion emerges as the J i ​ j = 0 J_{ij}=0 limit, and in Section IV , we show how restoring the physical couplings yields a structure-aware correlated framework.

sections.heading: III.  Diffusion as p-bit Dynamics
sections.text:
The forward process of diffusion where a structured state is progressively corrupted into noise is physically equivalent to heating a spin system toward infinite temperature. A network of p-bits with couplings J i ​ j J_{ij} sampling
from the Ising energy (Eq. 3 ) under a decreasing inverse temperature β \beta (Eq. 1 ) implements exactly this dynamics. We now show that the independent diffusion corresponds to the J i ​ j = 0 J_{ij}=0 limit of this process, and that restoring couplings yields the correlated framework developed in Section IV (Fig. 3 ).

sections.heading: IV.  Structure-Aware Correlated Diffusion
sections.text:
The equivalence established in the previous section invites an obvious generalization: if independent diffusion corresponds to J i ​ j = 0 J_{ij}=0 , what happens when the physical couplings are restored? For systems whose interaction structure is known, the factorized noise injection can be replaced with correlated MCMC dynamics that respect the underlying J i ​ j J_{ij} .

Consider the simplest interacting case: two spins coupled by J J .
The correlated forward process can be written exactly as a 4 × 4 4\times 4 transition matrix W Gibbs ​ ( β ) W_{\mathrm{Gibbs}}(\beta) , constructed as the product of per-spin transition matrices in a fixed update order (see Supplementary Section XIII ).
Because the matrix is small, quantities such as the prior and likelihood appearing in the reverse posterior (shown below in Eq. 8 ) can be computed by direct matrix multiplication.
For large N N , however, the full 2 N × 2 N 2^{N}\times 2^{N} transition matrix is intractable.
The scalable realization presented below replaces these exact matrix operations with Gibbs sampling using p-bit networks, where each spin is updated sequentially under its local field without ever constructing the full transition matrix.

sections.heading: V.  Results
sections.text: Prior work has applied diffusion to Ising-type systems [ 35 , 36 ] and proposed iterative neural network inference as a means of capturing conditional structure in the reverse process [ 37 ] , but in all cases, correlations are captured implicitly by the network rather than explicitly encoded in the diffusion process, as proposed here.

sections.heading: V.12D Ferromagnetic Ising Model
sections.text:
We first evaluate the proposed correlated diffusion framework on the 2D ferromagnetic Ising model on a 50 × 50 50\times 50 lattice with open boundary conditions at criticality.
The dataset consists of 10,000 equilibrium configurations collected via MCMC at zero external field and split 80/20 into training and validation sets.
Each s 0 s_{0} is propagated through 100 forward diffusion transitions to produce noisy states s t s_{t} at every timestep t ∈ { 1 , … , T } t\in\{1,\ldots,T\} , and a neural-network conditional estimator is trained on all resulting pairs ( s t , s 0 ) (s_{t},s_{0}) to predict per-site denoising probabilities.
During inference, 100 reverse-generation trajectories are produced using N chains = 10 N_{\mathrm{chains}}=10 candidate chains per reverse step, iterating the reverse kernel from a highly disordered s T s_{T} back to s 0 s_{0} .

Fig. 5 compares representative reverse trajectories for the independent and correlated kernels against Gibbs-sampled reference configurations at matching timesteps.
With a site-wise (independent) kernel, intermediate configurations exhibit spatially fragmented domains whose structure does not reflect the correlation length of the underlying Ising model at the corresponding temperature. In contrast, the interaction-aware (correlated) kernel produces a smoother evolution in which domains coarsen gradually as t t decreases, more closely resembling the Gibbs reference at each timestep.
The per-snapshot values of ⟨ m ⟩ \langle m\rangle and ⟨ E / N ⟩ \langle E/N\rangle reported beneath each configuration in Fig. 5 make this contrast quantitative: the correlated kernel tracks the reference values across timesteps, while the independent kernel can deviate in magnetization even when energy appears comparable.
A more detailed analysis of energy and magnetization across the full reverse trajectory is provided in Supplementary Section XV (Fig. S1 ), where the independent kernel is shown to develop a systematic magnetization bias that the correlated kernel avoids.

sections.heading: V.23D Spin Glass
sections.text:
The 2D ferromagnet, with its uniform couplings and long-range order, provides a clean first test, but the real question is whether correlated diffusion can handle systems with frustrated, disordered interactions.
We therefore apply the framework to the 3D Edwards-Anderson spin glass on a 10 × 10 × 10 10\times 10\times 10 cubic lattice ( N = 1000 N=1000 spins) with 100 forward diffusion transitions.
Here, random ± J \pm J couplings on a cubic lattice produce a rugged energy landscape with many competing low-energy states.
The dataset consists of 20,000 equilibrium configurations collected from a single disorder instance at criticality, split 80/20 into training and validation sets. We report 100 reverse-generation trajectories using N chains = 10 N_{\mathrm{chains}}=10 candidate chains per step.
The interaction matrix J i ​ j J_{ij} is directly incorporated into the p-bit Gibbs dynamics during both the forward and reverse processes.

Fig. 6 presents the results near the spin-glass phase transition.
The evolution of the mean energy per spin during the forward and reverse processes (Fig. 6 , top center) shows that the reverse trajectory tracks the forward energy profile, confirming that the learned reverse process produces states at physically appropriate energy scales throughout the denoising sequence.
The energy-per-spin distribution of the generated samples (Fig. 6 , top right) agrees with reference Monte Carlo data, indicating that the final generated configurations occupy the correct region of the energy landscape.

The most stringent test is the Parisi overlap distribution P ​ ( q ) P(q) (Fig. 6 , bottom right), where q = ( 1 / N ) ​ ∑ i s i ( α ) ​ s i ( β ) q=(1/N)\sum_{i}s_{i}^{(\alpha)}s_{i}^{(\beta)} measures the similarity between two independently generated configurations α \alpha and β \beta .
Unlike energy, which is a single-configuration observable, P ​ ( q ) P(q) probes the statistical relationships across the generated ensemble: reproducing its characteristic spread near the phase transition requires that the model captures the coexistence of many equilibrium states with distinct overlap structure.
Agreement with the reference P ​ ( q ) P(q) therefore confirms that correlated diffusion preserves the global structure of the spin-glass equilibrium, not just local energetics.

Together, these results show that correlated diffusion with physically informed Gibbs dynamics generates equilibrium-quality samples even in frustrated, high-dimensional systems where the energy landscape and the structure of the equilibrium state space are fundamentally more complex than in ordered magnets.

sections.heading: VI.  Conclusion
sections.text:
We have shown that independent diffusion is a special case of a more general framework in which the stochastic sampling component incorporates the interaction structure of the target system. The key observation is that independent diffusion corresponds to p-bit dynamics with J i ​ j = 0 J_{ij}=0 : each site undergoes a temperature sweep under a time-dependent local bias, and the resulting 2 × 2 2\times 2 kernels can be exponentiated analytically. Restoring the physical couplings J i ​ j ≠ 0 J_{ij}\neq 0 replaces this factorized process with correlated Gibbs dynamics over the full Ising Hamiltonian, yielding a structure-aware diffusion framework in which the stochastic component respects known spatial correlations at every step.

On two benchmark systems, the 2D ferromagnetic Ising model and the 3D Edwards-Anderson spin glass, correlated diffusion produces samples that track MCMC reference distributions more closely than independent diffusion in both local observables (energy, magnetization) and global ensemble statistics (the Parisi overlap P ​ ( q ) P(q) ). These gains come from encoding physical knowledge directly into the diffusion dynamics rather than relying on the neural network to learn all correlations implicitly.

The framework naturally maps onto probabilistic computers built from p-bits, where the correlated Gibbs sweeps that independent diffusion avoids become the native operation. FPGA-based p-computers already provide ∼ 10 2 × \sim\!10^{2}\times the sampling efficiency of GPUs, with projected sMTJ implementations reaching ∼ 10 4 × \sim\!10^{4}\times . As correlated diffusion shifts computational weight toward sampling, these hardware advantages become directly relevant.

Several directions remain open. The current reverse process relies on importance-weighted selection from a finite ensemble of Gibbs chains; more efficient sampling strategies, such as parallel tempering or learned proposal distributions, could reduce the number of chains required. The conditional estimator used here is a simple MLP: architectures that exploit spatial structure (e.g., graph neural networks) may further improve denoising accuracy.

Finally, extending the framework beyond equilibrium Ising systems to non-equilibrium dynamics, continuous-state models via g-bits, or systems where the couplings J i ​ j J_{ij} are partially known or must be inferred presents a natural next step. More broadly, the results suggest that the division of labor between neural-network estimation and structured stochastic sampling is a design axis that has been underexplored in the diffusion literature, and that dedicated sampling hardware can make previously impractical points along this axis accessible.

sections.heading: Author contributions
sections.text: N.S.S. and K.Y.C. conceived the study. N.S.S. led the development of the correlated diffusion framework, performed the main numerical experiments, and wrote the initial version of the manuscript. M.M.R. contributed to the 2D ferromagnetic Ising benchmarks and to the correlated diffusion formulation in support of the main development. S.N. contributed to the hardware benchmarking analysis, including the FPGA-related evaluation. All authors discussed the results. N.S.S. and K.Y.C. finalized the manuscript.

sections.heading: Competing interests
sections.text: The authors declare no competing interests.

sections.heading: Data availability
sections.text: Equilibrium samples for the 2D ferromagnetic Ising model and the 3D Edwards–Anderson spin glass at criticality used in this study are available at https://github.com/OPUSLab/CorrelatedDiffusion .

sections.heading: Code availability
sections.text: Code for training and inference of both independent and correlated diffusion on the 2D ferromagnetic Ising model, and correlated diffusion on the 3D spin glass, including data loading, noising, training, inference, and metrics evaluation, is available at https://github.com/OPUSLab/CorrelatedDiffusion .

sections.heading: VII.  g-bit formulation
sections.text: This section details the g-bit framework: Section VII.1 derives the Gibbs sampling equations for inter-g-bit interactions, and Section VII.2 describes the internal p-bit representation of individual g-bits.

sections.heading: VII.1Inter g-bit interactions
sections.text:
The p-bit energy is given by

E ​ ( m ) = − ∑ i < j J i ​ j ​ m i ​ m j − ∑ i h i ​ m i E(m)=-\sum_{i<j}J_{ij}m_{i}m_{j}-\sum_{i}h_{i}m_{i} (S.1)

similarly, when we work with g-bits, i.e., units that can represent multiple states that are gaussian-distributed, we have the energy given by

E ​ ( g ) = ∑ i ( g i − b i ) 2 2 ​ σ i 2 − ∑ i < j W i ​ j ​ g i ​ g j σ i ​ σ j E(g)=\sum_{i}\frac{(g_{i}-b_{i})^{2}}{2\sigma_{i}^{2}}-\sum_{i<j}\frac{W_{ij}g_{i}g_{j}}{\sigma_{i}\sigma_{j}} (S.2)

This section focuses on the Gibbs Sampling equations that approximate the energy terms corresponding to the Gaussian-Gaussian self and interaction terms.
Here we revisit our energy ansatz for the all g-bit network (all-Gaussian all-visible Boltzmann machine) which we will reference here as S.2 .

Here, we don’t double count interactions, i.e., if the interaction between g 1 g_{1} and g 2 g_{2} is considered, we don’t include the identical term for the g 2 g_{2} and g 1 g_{1} interaction. Next, we consider the ratio of π ​ ( g β ) \pi(g_{\beta}) to π ​ ( g α ) \pi(g_{\alpha}) , where π ​ ( g β ) \pi(g_{\beta}) and π ​ ( g α ) \pi(g_{\alpha}) are the probabilities of two configurations of the N N -unit visible system. In these configurations, only the unit g k g_{k} has a different real value in between configurations, with g k = g α g_{k}=g_{\alpha} in the first configuration, and g k = g β g_{k}=g_{\beta} in the second configuration. We then apply the rule of detailed balance

P ​ ( g α → g β ∣ g 1 , g 2 , … , g n ) P ​ ( g β → g α ∣ g 1 , g 2 , … , g n ) = π ​ ( g β ) π ​ ( g α ) \frac{P(g_{\alpha}\rightarrow g_{\beta}\mid g_{1},g_{2},\ldots,g_{n})}{P(g_{\beta}\rightarrow g_{\alpha}\mid g_{1},g_{2},\ldots,g_{n})}=\frac{\pi(g_{\beta})}{\pi(g_{\alpha})} (S.3)

To devise a sampling algorithm, we are interested in π ​ ( g β ) π ​ ( g α ) \frac{\pi(g_{\beta})}{\pi(g_{\alpha})} , which is given by S.4 .

e − E ​ ( g k = g β ∣ g 1 , g 2 , … , g n ) e − E ​ ( g k = g α ∣ g 1 , g 2 , … , g n ) \frac{e^{-E(g_{k}=g_{\beta}\mid g_{1},g_{2},\ldots,g_{n})}}{e^{-E(g_{k}=g_{\alpha}\mid g_{1},g_{2},\ldots,g_{n})}} (S.4)

Expression S.4 simplifies to the form e − Δ ​ E e^{-\Delta E} where − Δ ​ E -\Delta E is expressed as

− Δ ​ E = ( ( g α − b k ) 2 2 ​ σ k 2 ) − ( ( g β − b k ) 2 2 ​ σ k 2 ) + ( σ k ​ ∑ j W k ​ j ​ g j σ j ) ​ ( g β − g α σ k 2 ) -\Delta E=\left(\frac{(g_{\alpha}-b_{k})^{2}}{2\sigma_{k}^{2}}\right)-\left(\frac{(g_{\beta}-b_{k})^{2}}{2\sigma_{k}^{2}}\right)+\left(\sigma_{k}\sum_{j}\frac{W_{kj}g_{j}}{\sigma_{j}}\right)\left(\frac{g_{\beta}-g_{\alpha}}{\sigma_{k}^{2}}\right) (S.5)

We let z k = σ k ​ ∑ j W k ​ j ​ g j σ j z_{k}=\sigma_{k}\sum_{j}\frac{W_{kj}g_{j}}{\sigma_{j}} , which is not to be confused with the Boltzmann normalization factor, also known as the partition function. Further simplification steps are shown in S.6 , S.7 , S.8 , and S.9 .

− Δ ​ E = 1 2 ​ σ k 2 ​ [ ( g α − b k ) 2 − ( g β − b k ) 2 + 2 ​ ( g β − g α ) ​ z k ] -\Delta E=\frac{1}{2\sigma_{k}^{2}}\left[(g_{\alpha}-b_{k})^{2}-(g_{\beta}-b_{k})^{2}+2(g_{\beta}-g_{\alpha})z_{k}\right] (S.6)

− Δ ​ E = 1 2 ​ σ k 2 ​ [ g α 2 − g β 2 − 2 ​ g α ​ b k + 2 ​ g β ​ b k + 2 ​ ( g β − g α ) ​ z k ] -\Delta E=\frac{1}{2\sigma_{k}^{2}}\left[g_{\alpha}^{2}-g_{\beta}^{2}-2g_{\alpha}b_{k}+2g_{\beta}b_{k}+2(g_{\beta}-g_{\alpha})z_{k}\right] (S.7)

− Δ E = 1 2 ​ σ k 2 [ ( g α 2 − 2 g α ( b k + z k ) + ( b k + z k ) 2 ) ] − ( g β 2 − 2 g β ( b k + z k ) + ( b k + z k ) 2 ) ] -\Delta E=\frac{1}{2\sigma_{k}^{2}}\left[(g_{\alpha}^{2}-2g_{\alpha}(b_{k}+z_{k})+(b_{k}+z_{k})^{2})]-(g_{\beta}^{2}-2g_{\beta}(b_{k}+z_{k})+(b_{k}+z_{k})^{2})\right] (S.8)

∴ P ​ ( g α → g β ∣ g 1 , g 2 , … , g n ) P ​ ( g β → g α ∣ g 1 , g 2 , … , g n ) = exp ​ ( − ( g β − b k − z k ) 2 2 ​ σ k 2 ) exp ​ ( − ( g α − b k − z k ) 2 2 ​ σ k 2 ) = e − Δ ​ E \therefore\frac{P(g_{\alpha}\rightarrow g_{\beta}\mid g_{1},g_{2},\ldots,g_{n})}{P(g_{\beta}\rightarrow g_{\alpha}\mid g_{1},g_{2},\ldots,g_{n})}=\frac{\text{exp}(\frac{-(g_{\beta}-b_{k}-z_{k})^{2}}{2\sigma_{k}^{2}})}{\text{exp}(\frac{-(g_{\alpha}-b_{k}-z_{k})^{2}}{2\sigma_{k}^{2}})}=e^{-\Delta E} (S.9)

From Equation S.9 , we derive that each P ​ ( g α → g β ) P(g_{\alpha}\rightarrow g_{\beta}) can be expressed using the probability density of a Gaussian distribution of the form shown in Equation S.10 .
We get a final expression for the mean and sigma as shown in Equation S.11 .

P ​ ( g α → g β ) = 1 2 ​ π ​ σ 2 ​ e − ( x − μ ) 2 2 ​ σ 2 P(g_{\alpha}\rightarrow g_{\beta})=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^{2}}}e^{-\frac{(x-\mu)^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}} (S.10)

μ \displaystyle\mu = b k + ( σ k ​ ∑ j W k ​ j ​ g j σ j ) , \displaystyle=b_{k}+\left(\sigma_{k}\sum_{j}\frac{W_{kj}g_{j}}{\sigma_{j}}\right), σ \displaystyle\sigma = σ k \displaystyle=\sigma_{k} (S.11)

Based on the expressions for the conditional probabilities, we frame a pair of Gibbs sampling equations. The first equation samples g i g_{i} from the normal distribution with mean μ i \mu_{i} and standard deviation σ i \sigma_{i} . The second equation determines the value of μ i \mu_{i} based on Equation S.11 to give the final iterative Gibbs sampling equations Equations S.12 and S.13 .

g i = 𝒩 ​ ( I i , σ i 2 ) g_{i}=\mathcal{N}(I_{i},\sigma_{i}^{2}) (S.12)

I i = b i + σ i ​ ∑ j W i ​ j ​ g j σ j I_{i}=b_{i}+\sigma_{i}\sum_{j}\frac{W_{ij}g_{j}}{\sigma_{j}} (S.13)

Analogous to the inverse-temperature schedule used for p-bits, g-bit networks can be annealed by scaling the variance with β \beta : σ i ← σ i / β \sigma_{i}\leftarrow\sigma_{i}/\sqrt{\beta} , so that decreasing β \beta broadens the sampling distribution toward the high-temperature (noisy) limit.

sections.heading: VII.2G-bit internal representation
sections.text:
A g-bit consists of a network of N N interconnected p-bits. The energy of this Ising model is expressed as: E = − E 0 ​ ( 1 2 ​ 𝒎 T ​ J ​ 𝒎 + 𝒉 T ​ 𝒎 ) E=-E_{0}\left(\frac{1}{2}\boldsymbol{m}^{T}J\boldsymbol{m}+\boldsymbol{h}^{T}\boldsymbol{m}\right) (S.14)

Similar to the formulation described in Ref. [ 38 ] , we construct a random number “ G G ” from the outputs of the p-bits, given by:

G = 𝒅 T ​ 𝒎 G=\boldsymbol{d}^{T}\boldsymbol{m} (S.15)

where 𝒎 \boldsymbol{m} is the column vector of the N N p-bit states and

𝒅 = { 2 a − 1 , 2 a − 2 , … , 2 0 , 2 − 1 , 2 − 2 , … , 2 − b } \boldsymbol{d}=\{2^{a-1},\,2^{a-2},\,\ldots,\,2^{0},\,2^{-1},\,2^{-2},\,\ldots,\,2^{-b}\} (S.16)

We construct X X as a mean-zero univariate Gaussian based on G G , given by:

X = G − μ σ X=\frac{G-\mu}{\sigma} (S.17)

X = a ′ ​ ( 𝒅 T ​ 𝒎 ) + b ′ X=a^{\prime}(\boldsymbol{d}^{T}\boldsymbol{m})+b^{\prime} (S.18)

where the constants a ′ a^{\prime} and b ′ b^{\prime} are given by

a ′ = 1 σ a^{\prime}=\frac{1}{\sigma} (S.19) b ′ = − μ σ b^{\prime}=\frac{-\mu}{\sigma} (S.20)

The matrix version of 𝒅 \boldsymbol{d} , denoted as D D , is used to set J J , which is the interaction between the p-bits comprising the g-bit. D D is the outer product of 𝒅 \boldsymbol{d} with the diagonals set to 0. The derivation for J J and 𝒉 \boldsymbol{h} follows from Ref. [ 38 ] , with 𝒉 \boldsymbol{h} being the bias vector of the p-bit network. J = − D σ 2 J=-\frac{D}{\sigma^{2}} (S.21) 𝒉 = μ ​ 𝒅 σ 2 \boldsymbol{h}=\frac{\mu\boldsymbol{d}}{\sigma^{2}} (S.22)

It is important to note that, unlike the continuous variable model which uses unipolar binary variables consistent with standard GBM architecture, the fixed-precision p-bit representation employs bipolar variables for all p-bits. Conversion between unipolar and bipolar variables can be easily made depending on the chosen mapping for the problem.

sections.heading: VIII.  FPGA Architecture
sections.text:
The probabilistic computing architecture was implemented on an Xilinx Alveo U250 Data Center Accelerator Card using a custom digital design.

Each p-bit is implemented using three main components: a pseudo-random number generator (PRNG), a nonlinear activation block, and a comparator. The PRNG produces uniformly distributed random numbers that introduce stochasticity into the p-bit updates. The activation function is implemented using a lookup table (LUT) that approximates the hyperbolic tangent function. The weighted input to each p-bit is computed using a multiply–accumulate (MAC) unit that sums the contributions from neighboring p-bit states and bias terms. The resulting activation value is compared with the random number to generate the updated binary p-bit state. The details of the architecture can be found in Ref [ 11 ] .

For efficient digital realization, the bipolar p-bit representation
( m i ∈ { − 1 , 1 } m_{i}\in\{-1,1\} ) is mapped to a binary representation ( m i ∈ { 0 , 1 } m_{i}\in\{0,1\} ).
The coupling weights and bias terms are correspondingly transformed before being loaded onto the FPGA. To exploit hardware parallelism, p-bits are grouped using a graph-coloring scheme such that nodes within the same color can be updated simultaneously.
Communication between the host and FPGA is performed through a PCIe interface with a memory-mapped AXI register structure.

In this work, the FPGA p-computer is used to generate equilibrium training configurations via Gibbs sampling of the target Ising Hamiltonian.

sections.heading: X.1GPU numbers
sections.text:
The GPU numbers reported in Table 1 (main text) are based on our own experiments on an NVIDIA A100-SXM4-80GB GPU, informed by several literature sources. Ref. [ 32 ] describes how different GPU pseudo-random number generators (PRNGs) are emulated and implemented on hardware. Ref. [ 31 ] explicitly reports Gsamples/s throughput numbers for NVIDIA A100 GPUs. Diffusion workloads are typically compute-bound rather than memory-bound [ 39 ] , which allows power consumption to be estimated from compute-intensive benchmarks; Ref. [ 30 ] provides power consumption data for A100 GPUs under such workloads. Together, these references form the basis for the literature-backed GPU estimate reported in Table S1 ( 50 50 – 300 300 Gsamples/s throughput, 200 200 – 400 400 W power). Our experimental numbers are consistent in order of magnitude with these literature values and represent a best-case, fabric-local scenario on the A100. Both sets of numbers are listed in Table S1 .

Our experimental benchmark consists of a CUDA kernel in which each GPU thread maintains an independent 32-bit Galois linear feedback shift register (LFSR) whose state is stored entirely in registers. During kernel execution, each thread repeatedly advances its local LFSR and consumes the generated 32-bit samples within a tight loop, without writing any intermediate values to global or shared memory. This design intentionally eliminates memory traffic, ensuring that the measured throughput reflects local compute capability rather than memory bandwidth or host–device communication overhead.

A total of 552,960 concurrent threads are launched (2160 blocks with 256 threads per block), each generating 200,000 samples per iteration over 50 kernel iterations. Kernel execution time is measured using CUDA events, yielding an aggregate throughput of approximately 1.01 × 10 12 1.01\times 10^{12} 32-bit samples per second. GPU power consumption is measured concurrently during the timed region by asynchronously sampling the on-board power sensors at 100 ms intervals, and the reported power corresponds to the time-averaged draw over the kernel execution window. The benchmark sustains an average power consumption of approximately 283 W, resulting in an energy efficiency of approximately 3.56 Gsamples/J. Since this represents a best-case fabric-local RNG scenario on a GPU, we use these experimental numbers as the GPU baseline in Table 1 , providing the most favorable comparison point for the GPU and thus a conservative estimate of the FPGA and sMTJ improvement factors.

sections.heading: X.2FPGA numbers
sections.text:
The FPGA numbers reported in Table 1 are derived from the p-computer architecture described in Section VIII .
To isolate the random number generation capability, we strip away all logic, LUT, and MAC unit, and retain only the 32-bit LFSR-based pseudo-random number generators.
The architecture supports 10 5 10^{5} independent p-bits (Table S1 ), each containing its own LFSR, clocked at 150 MHz.
Each LFSR produces one 32-bit sample per clock cycle, yielding an aggregate throughput of 10 5 × 150 × 10 6 = 1.5 × 10 13 10^{5}\times 150\times 10^{6}=1.5\times 10^{13} samples/s, or approximately 15,000 Gsamples/s.
At a board power of 25 W, this gives an energy efficiency of 600 Gsamples/J, approximately 10 2 × 10^{2}\times the GPU baseline (Table 1 ).

sections.heading: XI.1Forward Process
sections.text:
Each site carries a spin s ∈ { − 1 , + 1 } s\in\{-1,+1\} . At diffusion step t t , the spin is flipped with probability η t \eta_{t} and kept with probability 1 − η t 1-\eta_{t} . Defining a t = 1 − 2 ​ η t a_{t}=1-2\eta_{t} and the cumulative product λ t = ∏ k = 1 t a k \lambda_{t}=\prod_{k=1}^{t}a_{k} , the cumulative kernel from time 0 to t t is

P ​ ( s t = s 0 ∣ s 0 ) \displaystyle P(s_{t}=s_{0}\mid s_{0}) = 1 2 ​ ( 1 + λ t ) , \displaystyle=\tfrac{1}{2}(1+\lambda_{t}), (S.23) P ​ ( s t = − s 0 ∣ s 0 ) \displaystyle P(s_{t}=-s_{0}\mid s_{0}) = 1 2 ​ ( 1 − λ t ) . \displaystyle=\tfrac{1}{2}(1-\lambda_{t}).

As t t increases, λ t → 0 \lambda_{t}\to 0 and both probabilities approach 1 2 \tfrac{1}{2} , corresponding to complete randomization. The two cases above can be written compactly as P ​ ( s t ∣ s 0 ) = 1 2 ​ [ 1 + λ t ​ s t ​ s 0 ] P(s_{t}\mid s_{0})=\tfrac{1}{2}\big[1+\lambda_{t}s_{t}s_{0}\big] (S.24)

sections.heading: XI.2One-Step Reverse Posterior
sections.text:
The one-step reverse posterior follows from Bayes’ rule: P ​ ( s t − 1 ∣ s t , s 0 ) = P ​ ( s t ∣ s t − 1 , s 0 ) ​ P ​ ( s t − 1 ∣ s 0 ) P ​ ( s t ∣ s 0 ) P(s_{t-1}\mid s_{t},s_{0})=\frac{P(s_{t}\mid s_{t-1},s_{0})\,P(s_{t-1}\mid s_{0})}{P(s_{t}\mid s_{0})} (S.25)

By the Markov property of the forward step, P ​ ( s t ∣ s t − 1 , s 0 ) = P ​ ( s t ∣ s t − 1 ) P(s_{t}\mid s_{t-1},s_{0})=P(s_{t}\mid s_{t-1}) .
Using the one-step channel, P ​ ( s t ∣ s t − 1 ) = 1 2 ​ [ 1 + a t ​ s t ​ s t − 1 ] P(s_{t}\mid s_{t-1})=\tfrac{1}{2}\big[1+a_{t}s_{t}s_{t-1}\big] (S.26)

and the cumulative kernel up to t − 1 t-1 , P ​ ( s t − 1 ∣ s 0 ) = 1 2 ​ [ 1 + λ t − 1 ​ s t − 1 ​ s 0 ] P(s_{t-1}\mid s_{0})=\tfrac{1}{2}\big[1+\lambda_{t-1}s_{t-1}s_{0}\big] (S.27)

we compute the probability for the event s t − 1 = + 1 s_{t-1}=+1 (the other case is symmetric): P ​ ( s t − 1 = + 1 ∣ s t , s 0 ) = 1 2 ​ [ 1 + a t ​ s t + λ t − 1 ​ s 0 1 + a t ​ λ t − 1 ​ s t ​ s 0 ] P(s_{t-1}=+1\mid s_{t},s_{0})=\tfrac{1}{2}\left[1+\frac{a_{t}s_{t}+\lambda_{t-1}s_{0}}{1+a_{t}\lambda_{t-1}s_{t}s_{0}}\right] (S.28)

sections.heading: XII.  Connecting Flip Probability (η\eta) to Inverse Temperature (β\beta)
sections.text:
In independent diffusion models, the noising process involves updating a spin with spin-flip probability η t − 1 \eta_{t-1} to transition from s i t − 1 s_{i}^{t-1} to s i t s_{i}^{t} . This can be written as follows:

Pr ⁡ ( s i t = − s i t − 1 ∣ s i t − 1 ) = η t − 1 \displaystyle\Pr\big(s_{i}^{t}=-s_{i}^{t-1}\mid s_{i}^{t-1})=\eta_{t-1} (S.29) Pr ⁡ ( s i t = s i t − 1 ∣ s i t − 1 ) = 1 − η t − 1 \displaystyle\Pr\big(s_{i}^{t}=s_{i}^{t-1}\mid s_{i}^{t-1})=1-\eta_{t-1}

Now, since this is an independent noising model, if we were to design a p-bit network that performs an analogous noising process, we enforce the following condition:

I i , ind t = β i t − 1 ​ h i t − 1 ​ s i t − 1 I_{i,\text{ind}}^{t}=\beta_{i}^{t-1}h_{i}^{t-1}s_{i}^{t-1} (S.30)

Since we refer to an independent noising model, by definition, I i , ind I_{i,\text{ind}} cannot depend on other s j s_{j} . This essentially then forms a feedback, where the output of the p-bit is taken and fed back to the input after scaling by h i h_{i} .

Now, we try to determine how to compute h i t − 1 h_{i}^{t-1} given a flip probability η t − 1 \eta_{t-1} . We know that the p-bit flipping probability can be expressed in terms of our expression for I i , ind I_{i,\text{ind}} as follows with s ∈ − 1 , + 1 s\in{-1,+1} :

Pr ⁡ ( s i t = s ∣ s i t − 1 ) = 1 + s ​ tanh ⁡ ( β i t − 1 ​ h i t − 1 ​ s i t − 1 ) 2 \Pr\big(s_{i}^{t}=s\mid s_{i}^{t-1}\big)=\frac{1+s\tanh(\beta_{i}^{t-1}h_{i}^{t-1}s_{i}^{t-1})}{2} (S.31)

Therefore,

Pr ⁡ ( s i t = + 1 ∣ s i t − 1 = − 1 ) = 1 − tanh ⁡ ( β i t − 1 ​ h i t − 1 ) 2 \Pr\big(s_{i}^{t}=+1\mid s_{i}^{t-1}=-1\big)=\frac{1-\tanh(\beta_{i}^{t-1}h_{i}^{t-1})}{2} (S.32)

Similarly,

Pr ⁡ ( s i t = − 1 ∣ s 1 t − 1 = + 1 ) = 1 − tanh ⁡ ( β i t − 1 ​ h i t − 1 ) 2 \Pr\big(s_{i}^{t}=-1\mid s_{1}^{t-1}=+1\big)=\frac{1-\tanh(\beta_{i}^{t-1}h_{i}^{t-1})}{2} (S.33)

Alternatively, we can write the flip event as ( s = − s i t − 1 s=-s_{i}^{t-1} ) follows: Pr ⁡ ( s i t = − s i t − 1 ∣ s i t − 1 ) \displaystyle\Pr(s_{i}^{t}=-s_{i}^{t-1}\mid s_{i}^{t-1}) = 1 + ( − s i t − 1 ) ​ tanh ⁡ ( β i t − 1 ​ h i t − 1 ​ s i t − 1 ) 2 \displaystyle=\frac{1+(-s_{i}^{t-1})\tanh(\beta_{i}^{t-1}h_{i}^{t-1}s_{i}^{t-1})}{2} (S.34) = 1 − tanh ⁡ ( β i t − 1 ​ h i t − 1 ) 2 . \displaystyle=\frac{1-\tanh(\beta_{i}^{t-1}h_{i}^{t-1})}{2}.

Now, we can equate this to η \eta and determine the expression for h i h_{i} based on this.

η t − 1 = 1 − tanh ⁡ ( β i t − 1 ​ h i t − 1 ) 2 \eta_{t-1}=\frac{1-\tanh(\beta_{i}^{t-1}h_{i}^{t-1})}{2} (S.35)

Here, we can drop the subscript i i

β t − 1 ​ h = arctanh ⁡ ( 1 − 2 ​ η t − 1 ) \beta_{t-1}h=\operatorname{arctanh}(1-2\eta_{t-1}) (S.36)

Now, although β t \beta_{t} and h t h_{t} both appear, only one needs to be set based on η \eta and the other can remain fixed. Thus, we can frame this as a beta schedule entirely with a fixed bias ( h t = 1 h_{t}=1 ).

β t − 1 = arctanh ⁡ ( 1 − 2 ​ η t − 1 ) \beta_{t-1}=\operatorname{arctanh}(1-2\eta_{t-1}) (S.37)

Since in standard Bernoulli diffusion models η t − 1 \eta_{t-1} (time-dependent, monotonically increasing) typically increases from 0 (no noise) to 0.5 0.5 (complete randomization), the corresponding inverse temperature ( β \beta ) varies as:

β t − 1 = arctanh ⁡ ( 1 − 2 ​ η t − 1 ) ∈ [ 0 , + ∞ ) , \beta_{t-1}=\operatorname{arctanh}(1-2\eta_{t-1})\in[0,+\infty), (S.38)

indicating a progressively increasing noising process.

sections.heading: XIII.  Correlated Diffusion Model: Transition Matrix Approach
sections.text: This section presents the formulation for correlated diffusion using explicit transition matrices. While exact, this approach is limited to small systems due to the 2 N × 2 N 2^{N}\times 2^{N} matrix size.

sections.heading: XIII.1Forward Process for Correlated Noising
sections.text:
Let us consider two spins with a + J J coupling between them, and the energy of the pair given by E ​ ( s ) = − J ​ s 1 ​ s 2 E(s)=-Js_{1}s_{2} where J > 0 J>0 .

To perform noising or reverse annealing, during the forward process, as we move from s 0 s_{0} to s t s_{t} , we decrease the value of beta to allow for a steady degradation of the state to noise.

A standard Gibbs sampling update for spin i i given the other spin j j is:

Pr ⁡ ( s i ′ = + 1 ∣ s j ) = 1 + tanh ⁡ ( β ​ J ​ s j ) 2 = 1 + s j ​ tanh ⁡ ( β ​ J ) 2 . \Pr(s_{i}^{\prime}=+1\mid s_{j})=\frac{1+\tanh(\beta Js_{j})}{2}=\frac{1+s_{j}\tanh(\beta J)}{2}. (S.39)

So, if the neighbor is +1, Pr ⁡ ( s i ′ = + 1 ) = q \Pr(s_{i}^{\prime}=+1)=q and Pr ⁡ ( s i ′ = − 1 ) = p \Pr(s_{i}^{\prime}=-1)=p .

Similarly, if the neighbor is -1, Pr ⁡ ( s i ′ = + 1 ) = p \Pr(s_{i}^{\prime}=+1)=p and Pr ⁡ ( s i ′ = − 1 ) = q \Pr(s_{i}^{\prime}=-1)=q .

Now, we form a full step as first updating s 1 s_{1} given s 2 s_{2} , then updating s 2 s_{2} given the possibly changed s 1 s_{1} .

(For setting the matrix, let rows and columns go (-1,-1), (-1,+1), (+1,-1), (+1,+1), with MSB being s 1 s_{1} and LSB being s 2 s_{2} .)

First, we update s 1 s_{1} given s 2 s_{2} :

W ​ ( β ) 1 ← 2 = [ q 0 q 0 0 p 0 p p 0 p 0 0 q 0 q ] . W(\beta)_{\text{1 $\leftarrow$ 2}}=\begin{bmatrix}q&0&q&0\\
0&p&0&p\\
p&0&p&0\\
0&q&0&q\end{bmatrix}. (S.40)

Next, we update s 2 s_{2} given s 1 s_{1} :

W ​ ( β ) 2 ← 1 = [ q q 0 0 p p 0 0 0 0 p p 0 0 q q ] . W(\beta)_{\text{2 $\leftarrow$ 1}}=\begin{bmatrix}q&q&0&0\\
p&p&0&0\\
0&0&p&p\\
0&0&q&q\end{bmatrix}. (S.41)

Now, to evaluate the composite transition matrix across both update steps, we get W ​ ( β ) Gibbs W(\beta)_{\text{Gibbs}} as follows.

W ​ ( β ) Gibbs = W ​ ( β ) 2 ← 1 ​ W ​ ( β ) 1 ← 2 W(\beta)_{\text{Gibbs}}=W(\beta)_{\text{2 $\leftarrow$ 1}}W(\beta)_{\text{1 $\leftarrow$ 2}} (S.42)

W ​ ( β ) Gibbs = [ q 2 p ​ q q 2 p ​ q p ​ q p 2 p ​ q p 2 p 2 p ​ q p 2 p ​ q p ​ q q 2 p ​ q q 2 ] . W(\beta)_{\text{Gibbs}}=\begin{bmatrix}q^{2}&pq&q^{2}&pq\\
pq&p^{2}&pq&p^{2}\\
p^{2}&pq&p^{2}&pq\\
pq&q^{2}&pq&q^{2}\end{bmatrix}. (S.43)

The forward process is defined using the diffusion-timestep-dependent W Gibbs W_{\mathrm{Gibbs}} (note that timestep dependence is equivalent to β \beta dependence, since the β \beta schedule varies with time):

s 0 ​ is the initial 2-spin state , \displaystyle s_{0}\text{ is the initial 2-spin state}, (S.44) 𝐯 0 = 𝐨𝐧𝐞𝐡𝐨𝐭 ​ ( s 0 ) \displaystyle\mathbf{v}_{0}=\mathbf{onehot}(s_{0}) For ​ t = 1 , 2 , … , T − 1 : \displaystyle\text{For }t=1,2,\ldots,T-1: π t = W Gibbs ​ ( β t − 1 ) ​ 𝐯 t − 1 \displaystyle\quad\pi_{t}=W_{\mathrm{Gibbs}}(\beta_{t-1})\mathbf{v}_{t-1} 𝐯 t ∼ π t \displaystyle\quad\mathbf{v}_{t}\sim\pi_{t}

sections.heading: XIII.2One-Step Reverse Posterior for Correlated Noising
sections.text:
We use the standard one-step Bayes formula to evaluate the posterior.

Pr ⁡ ( s t − 1 ∣ s t , s 0 ) = Pr ⁡ ( s t ∣ s t − 1 ) ​ Pr ⁡ ( s t − 1 ∣ s 0 ) Pr ⁡ ( s t ∣ s 0 ) . \Pr(s_{t-1}\mid s_{t},s_{0})=\frac{\Pr(s_{t}\mid s_{t-1})\,\Pr(s_{t-1}\mid s_{0})}{\Pr(s_{t}\mid s_{0})}. (S.45)

Pr ⁡ ( s t − 1 ∣ s t , s 0 ) = ℓ s t ∘ π t − 1 Z . \Pr(s_{t-1}\mid s_{t},s_{0})=\frac{\ell_{s_{t}}\circ\pi_{t-1}}{Z}. (S.46)

Now, with the determined W ​ ( β ) Gibbs W(\beta)_{\text{Gibbs}} we can look at how we compute the different terms in the Bayes formulation.

We can denote the likelihood by selecting the row from W ​ ( β ) Gibbs W(\beta)_{\text{Gibbs}} corresponding to the s t s_{t} value that we start the reverse process with. This gives us the likelihood of the observed s t s_{t} from some s t − 1 s_{t-1} .

L s t = W ​ ( β t − 1 ) Gibbs ​ [ s t , : ] L_{s_{t}}=W(\beta_{t-1})_{\text{Gibbs}}[s_{t},:] (S.47)

Pr ⁡ ( s t ∣ s t − 1 ) = ℓ s t = L s t T \Pr(s_{t}\mid s_{t-1})=\ell_{s_{t}}=L_{s_{t}}^{T} (S.48)

The prior is π t − 1 \pi_{t-1} obtained by pushing the given s 0 s_{0} through the forward chain for t − 1 t-1 steps. This forms our belief for s t − 1 s_{t-1} only given s 0 s_{0} and the forward noising schedule. 𝐯 0 \mathbf{v}_{0} in this case is the one-hot encoded version of s 0 s_{0} and has dimension of 4 × 1 4\times 1 , and since our transition matrix is 4 × 4 4\times 4 , the dimensions work out.

Pr ⁡ ( s t − 1 ∣ s 0 ) = π t − 1 \displaystyle\Pr(s_{t-1}\mid s_{0})=\pi_{t-1} = W ​ ( β t − 2 ) Gibbs × W ​ ( β t − 3 ) Gibbs ​ … \displaystyle=W(\beta_{t-2})_{\text{Gibbs}}\times W(\beta_{t-3})_{\text{Gibbs}}\dots (S.49) × W ​ ( β 1 ) Gibbs × W ​ ( β 0 ) Gibbs ​ 𝐯 0 \displaystyle\quad\times W(\beta_{1})_{\text{Gibbs}}\times W(\beta_{0})_{\text{Gibbs}}\,\mathbf{v}_{0}

The normalization is the total probability of seeing the current state s t s_{t} under the forward model and our belief about s 0 s_{0} .

Pr ⁡ ( s t ∣ s 0 ) \displaystyle\Pr(s_{t}\mid s_{0}) = [ π t ] s t \displaystyle=[\,\pi_{t}\,]_{s_{t}} (S.50) = [ W ( β t − 1 ) Gibbs × W ( β t − 2 ) Gibbs … \displaystyle=[\,W(\beta_{t-1})_{\text{Gibbs}}\times W(\beta_{t-2})_{\text{Gibbs}}\dots × W ( β 1 ) Gibbs × W ( β 0 ) Gibbs 𝐯 0 ] s t \displaystyle\quad\times W(\beta_{1})_{\text{Gibbs}}\times W(\beta_{0})_{\text{Gibbs}}\,\mathbf{v}_{0}\,]_{s_{t}}

The normalization factor can also be determined by the dot product of the previous two terms calculated, as shown below.

Z = L s t ⋅ π t − 1 = W ​ ( β t − 1 ) Gibbs ​ [ s t , : ] ⋅ π t − 1 \text{Z}=L_{s_{t}}\cdot\pi_{t-1}=W(\beta_{t-1})_{\text{Gibbs}}[s_{t},:]\cdot\pi_{t-1} (S.51)

So the final expression for the posterior can be given by the following:

Pr ⁡ ( s t − 1 ∣ s t , s 0 ) = W ​ ( β t − 1 ) Gibbs ​ [ s t , : ] T ∘ π t − 1 W ​ ( β t − 1 ) Gibbs ​ [ s t , : ] ⋅ π t − 1 \Pr(s_{t-1}\mid s_{t},s_{0})=\frac{W(\beta_{t-1})_{\text{Gibbs}}[s_{t},:]^{T}\circ\pi_{t-1}}{W(\beta_{t-1})_{\text{Gibbs}}[s_{t},:]\cdot\pi_{t-1}} (S.52)

sections.heading: XIV.  Network and Training Details
sections.text:
2D Ferromagnetic Ising Model: Equilibrium configurations for the 50 × 50 50\times 50 ferromagnetic Ising model were generated via single-spin-flip Gibbs sampling.
For each independent trial, the p-bit weights were first set to β = 0 \beta=0 (effectively randomizing the initial state), then updated to the critical inverse temperature and evolved for 10 5 10^{5} Gibbs sweeps, after which a single configuration was read out.
The 10,000 configurations used as the training dataset were collected from independent trials at the critical point.

3D Edwards-Anderson Spin Glass: Reference configurations for the 3D Edwards-Anderson spin glass were generated via single-spin-flip Gibbs sampling.
All chains were initialized in the uniform s i = + 1 s_{i}=+1 state.
Equilibration consisted of 10 6 10^{6} sweeps ramping linearly from β = 0 \beta=0 to the critical inverse temperature of the disorder instance, followed by 9 × 10 6 9\times 10^{6} sweeps at the critical point.
Final configurations after 10 7 10^{7} total sweeps at the critical point were used as the 20,000 training configurations referenced in the main text.

sections.heading: XV.  2D Ferromagnetic Ising Model: Extended Results
sections.text:
Fig. S1 extends the snapshot comparison in Fig. 5 by plotting ⟨ E ⟩ / N \langle E\rangle/N and | ⟨ m ⟩ | |\langle m\rangle| across the full reverse trajectory.
Under independent noising, the energy trajectory roughly tracks the forward reference (Fig. S1 a), but the magnetization develops a strong bias at small t t that is absent in the forward process (Fig. S1 b).
Under correlated noising, both observables track the MCMC reference closely across all timesteps (Fig. S1 c,d).

figures.caption:
Fig. 1 : Hybrid p-computer/GPU inference loop for correlated diffusion. System-level view of one reverse-diffusion iteration. The current noisy configuration ( s t s_{t} ) is passed from the probabilistic computer to the neural network on the GPU, which produces per-site denoising probabilities p = f θ ​ ( s t ) p=f_{\theta}(s_{t}) .
A binary clean-state estimate s ^ 0 \hat{s}_{0} is then sampled from these probabilities and sent back to the probabilistic computer, where it initializes an ensemble of forward Gibbs chains under the known couplings J i ​ j J_{ij} and the scheduled inverse temperatures.
Each chain produces a candidate for s t − 1 s_{t-1} , and the candidates are reweighted by the one-step likelihood P ​ ( s t ∣ s t − 1 ) P(s_{t}\mid s_{t-1}) before one is sampled to continue the reverse trajectory.
Repeating this procedure produces successive reverse states s t → s t − 1 → ⋯ → s 0 s_{t}\rightarrow s_{t-1}\rightarrow\cdots\rightarrow s_{0} , yielding the final sample s 0 s_{0} .

Fig. 2 : Probabilistic computing hardware primitives. (a) p-bits and g-bits serve as the building blocks for Bernoulli and Gaussian sampling, respectively.
(b) Multi-state Potts variables with integer states in [ 0 , 2 N − 1 ] [0,2^{N}-1] can be realized either as native Potts units or as networks of interconnected p-bits.
(c) A p-bit circuit and candidate stochastic nanodevice realizations, including sMTJs, Zener diodes, and SPADs.
(d) Three tunable g-bits implemented on FPGA, each with 256 states and independently programmable means and variances.

Fig. 3 : Independent and correlated diffusion noising in a p-bit framework: Illustration of the forward noising process implemented using probabilistic bits (p-bits), showing how structure-aware correlated noising generalizes the independent diffusion for a 2D ferromagnetic Ising system. (a) Independent diffusion noising : each p-bit s i s_{i} is updated with J i ​ j = 0 J_{ij}=0 under a time-dependent local bias h i = s i t − 1 h_{i}=s_{i}^{t-1} , so that I i = s i t − 1 I_{i}=s_{i}^{t-1} ; a decreasing inverse temperature schedule β → 0 \beta\to 0 (Eq. 1 ) progressively destroys correlations, resulting in factorized, site-wise noise injection (top row). (b) Correlated diffusion noising : each p-bit is updated from an interaction-dependent local field I i = ∑ j J i ​ j ​ s j + h i I_{i}=\sum_{j}J_{ij}s_{j}+h_{i} , where explicit couplings J i ​ j J_{ij} introduce spatially correlated noise consistent with the underlying Ising structure (bottom row). The independent noising process in (a) is recovered as a special case of (b) by setting J i ​ j = 0 J_{ij}=0 and absorbing the noising strength into the bias term h i h_{i} .

Fig. 4 : Reverse generative process (diffusion inference) using neural-network guidance and p-bit sampling: Schematic of one reverse step. Given the current noisy state s t s_{t} , a neural network computes per-site denoising probabilities p = f θ ​ ( s t ) p=f_{\theta}(s_{t}) , from which a clean-state estimate s ^ 0 \hat{s}_{0} is sampled. An ensemble of forward Gibbs chains initialized at s ^ 0 \hat{s}_{0} then produces candidates for s t − 1 s_{t-1} on the probabilistic computer. These candidates are reweighted according to Pr ⁡ ( s t − 1 ∣ s t , s ^ 0 ) ∝ Pr ⁡ ( s t ∣ s t − 1 ) ​ Pr ⁡ ( s t − 1 ∣ s ^ 0 ) \Pr(s_{t-1}\mid s_{t},\hat{s}_{0})\propto\Pr(s_{t}\mid s_{t-1})\,\Pr(s_{t-1}\mid\hat{s}_{0}) ,
and one candidate is sampled to continue the reverse trajectory. Repeating this procedure yields a sequence of progressively denoised states ending at s 0 s_{0} .

Fig. 5 : Reverse trajectories for independent and correlated kernels on the 2D ferromagnetic Ising model. Representative configurations (right-to-left) at diffusion timesteps t = { 100 , 55 , 33 , 11 , 0 } t=\{100,55,33,11,0\} (red/blue denote s = + 1 / − 1 s=+1/-1 ). Top: independent (site-wise) kernel. Middle: Gibbs-sampled reference at matching timesteps. Bottom: correlated (interaction-aware) kernel. The mean magnetization ⟨ m ⟩ \langle m\rangle and mean energy per spin ⟨ E ⟩ / N \langle E\rangle/N are reported beneath each snapshot. The correlated kernel tracks the Gibbs reference more closely across timesteps, while the independent kernel produces spatially fragmented intermediate states and can deviate in magnetization even when energy appears comparable.

Fig. 6 : Correlated diffusion applied to a 3D Edwards-Anderson spin glass near the phase transition. (Top left) Cubic lattice and ± J \pm J coupling instance. (Top center) Mean energy per spin during forward and reverse diffusion. (Top right) Energy-per-spin distribution of generated samples versus Monte Carlo reference. (Bottom left) Representative spin trajectories during diffusion. (Bottom center) Training and validation loss of the conditional estimator. (Bottom right) Parisi overlap distribution P ​ ( q ) P(q) of generated samples compared with the equilibrium reference, showing that the framework reproduces the characteristic overlap structure of the spin-glass phase.

Fig. S1 : Energy and magnetization across diffusion timesteps for generated Ising states. Mean energy per spin ⟨ E ⟩ / N \langle E\rangle/N and absolute mean magnetization | ⟨ m ⟩ | |\langle m\rangle| versus diffusion timestep t t . (a,b) Independent noising: reverse-generated states (orange/red) are compared with forward samples from the factorized noising kernel (blue/green). Energy appears broadly similar under a chosen schedule (a), but magnetization differs strongly (b), showing that independent noising suppresses long-range order too rapidly. (c,d) Correlated noising: reverse-generated states (orange/red) are compared with interaction-respecting MCMC reference states (blue/green). Both ⟨ E ⟩ / N \langle E\rangle/N and | ⟨ m ⟩ | |\langle m\rangle| track the reference more closely over t t , consistent with the preservation of spatial correlations in the forward kernel.
